name: US Manufacturing Real Unit Production Labor Cost Index

book_quotes.quote: real unit labor cost, the ratio of real wages to productivity, is of paramount importance to business.
Figure 2.6 US Manufacturing Real Unit Production Labor Cost Index, 1889–2010. Derived as the ratio of manufacturing real compensation and productivity in the previous chart.

primary_source.agency: Derived (Shaikh 2016) from BEA + BLS + MeasuringWorth components used in Fig 2.5
primary_source.publication: Shaikh (2016), Capitalism: Competition, Conflict, Crises, Appendix 2.1 (p. 764)
primary_source.table_or_series_id: Derived: real_compensation / productivity (both from S207 components)
primary_source.license: Derived; underlying sources are public domain + MeasuringWorth academic license
primary_source.available_through: FRED ULCMFG (modern BLS manufacturing unit labor cost) as a partial analogue

components.label: Manufacturing productivity index (S207 component)
components.source: BEA LTEG A173 + BLS FLS Table 1

extension_candidates.subseries_label: FRED ULCMFG (Manufacturing Sector: Unit Labor Cost)
concerns: ULCMFG is nominal ULC (compensation/output), not the deflated ratio Shaikh computes. For faithful extension, build the ratio from extended OPHMFG (productivity) and real compensation (FRED COMPRMS or MeasuringWorth).
Honors anu-framework no-lazy-splice rule for derived quantities — recompute formula with extended components rather than splicing the ratio directly.

methodology_notes: Formula series: explicit ratio of S207's two components (real compensation ÷ productivity).
CD2 S008 dossier provides the multi-column construction; final series is the index of the ratio.
Anu Framework principle: extension must recompute the formula with extended components, not splice the ratio.
open_questions: Confirm post-2013 BLS series for manufacturing real hourly compensation (CD2 noted BLS PRS30006152); some manufacturing-sector PRS series are discontinued.
